question: Three detection strategies for particle dark matter: name and contrast.

options: (1) Direct detection: DM elastic scattering off nuclei/electrons in shielded underground detectors (XENONnT, LZ, PandaX); (2) Indirect: search for SM products of DM annihilation/decay in cosmic rays, gammas, neutrinos (Fermi-LAT, IceCube); (3) Collider: produce DM at LHC, detect via missing transverse energy. Complementary: each probes different couplings and DM mass ranges

Only gravitational detection possible (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence)

Direct detection alone suffices (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)

Indirect detection looks for DM in cosmic microwave background (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Three pillars of DM searches. Direct: $\chi + N \to \chi + N$, recoil energy $\sim$ keV. Spin-independent (coherent) cross section grows as $A^2$ — heavy nuclei better; xenon (A = 131) preferred. Spin-dependent: $\propto$ nucleus spin, fluorine/iodine targets. Indirect: $\chi\chi \to f\bar f, \gamma\gamma, W^+W^-$. Look for spectral lines, dwarf galaxies, galactic centre, Sun. Collider: $pp \to \chi\bar\chi + X$, with $X$ = jet, photon, etc. — distinguishable from SM backgrounds by missing $E_T$. Tensions: positive signals (DAMA modulation, gamma-ray galactic centre excess, 511 keV line) not reproduced by other experiments — controversial.

active.prompt: Match each DM candidate type with its mass scale and search strategy:

pairs.definition: GeV-TeV; direct detection (XENONnT, LZ); thermal relic abundance natural in this range
$\mu$eV-meV; haloscope (ADMX, MADMAX), helioscope (CAST, IAXO); solves strong-CP
$10^{-22}$ eV; wavelength $\sim$ kpc; tests via pulsar timing, galactic structure, BH superradiance
$10^{-16}-10\,M_\odot$; microlensing (HSC, OGLE), GW events (LIGO subsolar), CMB constraints

active.explanation: DM mass range spans 80 orders of magnitude. No single experiment can cover all. WIMP miracle (thermal relic at weak-scale coupling) gives natural prediction at GeV-TeV; direct searches now reaching $10^{-47}$ cm$^2$ at $\sim 30$ GeV. Axions: 'natural' mass window broadens with new experiments. Multi-channel approach essential.

question: Direct detection: kinematics and the $A^2$ enhancement.

options: DM ($m_\chi \sim 100$ GeV, $v \sim 220$ km/s in galactic halo) elastically scatters off nucleus. Maximum recoil energy $E_R^{\max} = 2\mu^2 v^2/m_N$ with reduced mass $\mu$; typically $\sim 10$ keV for Xe target. Coherent SI scattering: nucleus seen as single object → cross section $\propto A^2$ (favours heavy nuclei). Annual modulation from Earth's velocity around Sun (relative to halo)
DM scattering produces MeV recoils (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence)
Cross section independent of nucleus type (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)

Direct detection looks for DM photons (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: DM velocity distribution: Maxwell-Boltzmann with $v_0 \approx 220$ km/s, $v_{\rm esc} \approx 540$ km/s (Standard Halo Model). Recoil energy: $E_R = (\mu^2 v^2/m_N)(1-\cos\theta_{CM})$, max at backscatter. For $m_\chi = 100$ GeV on Xe ($A = 131$): $\mu \approx 56$ GeV, $E_R^{\max} \approx 12$ keV. Coherent enhancement: spin-independent cross-section sums over all $A$ nucleons in phase below momentum transfer $q < 1/R_{\rm nuc}$ — $\sigma_{SI} \propto A^2 \mu^2$. Form factor $F^2(q)$ suppresses above $q R_{\rm nuc} > 1$ (typically $E_R \gtrsim 50$ keV). Annual modulation: $v_{\rm Earth} = v_{\rm halo} + v_{\rm orb}\cos[\omega(t - t_0)]$, signal amplitude $\sim 5\%$, period 1 year. Sole positive claim: DAMA/LIBRA at sodium iodide — unconfirmed by other materials/exposures.

question: Why are direct detection experiments built deep underground?

options: Cosmic ray background: $\sim 1$ muon per minute per cm² at sea level; produces nuclear recoils mimicking DM. Underground: $\sim$ km rock attenuates muon flux $10^6$-fold; radon, neutrons from rock managed with shielding (water, lead, copper) and active vetoes. Typical site: Gran Sasso (3800 m.w.e.), SURF (4300 m.w.e.), SNOLAB (6000 m.w.e.)
Underground is colder, reduces thermal noise (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

Deeper sites have stronger DM flux (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect)
Surface labs work equally well (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: DM signal rate: $\sim$ 0.001 event/kg/day for $\sigma \sim 10^{-46}$ cm$^2$. Cosmic muons at surface: $\sim 100$/m$^2$/s, would swamp signal. Depth shields exponentially: muon flux at 1 km.w.e.\ $\sim$ $10^{-2}$ surface; at 5 km.w.e.\ $\sim 10^{-7}$. Other backgrounds: radon ($^{222}$Rn from rock decay, mitigated by purification + clean materials), neutrons (from rock $(\alpha,n)$ + spontaneous fission, shielded by hydrogenous material like water or polyethylene), $\gamma$-rays (from rock and own materials, mitigated by pure construction + active veto). Active vetoes: scintillator outside detector tags coincident muons/neutrons. Major underground labs: Gran Sasso (Italy), SNOLAB (Canada, Sudbury), Sanford Underground Research Facility (US, South Dakota), Boulby (UK), Kamioka (Japan), CJPL (China, deepest at 2400 m of rock).

question: Indirect detection: what signatures distinguish DM annihilation from astrophysical backgrounds?

options: Spectral cuts: monochromatic gamma line at $E_\gamma = m_\chi$ from $\chi\chi\to\gamma\gamma$; no astrophysical source produces sharp lines. Sky morphology: dwarf spheroidal galaxies are DM-rich, low background; galactic centre profile predicts NFW or cored signal. Mass thresholds: thermal relic $\langle\sigma v\rangle \sim 3\times 10^{-26}$ cm$^3$/s benchmark. Constraints from Fermi-LAT, MAGIC, HESS, IceCube

Indirect detection has no distinguishing features (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general)

Only neutrino flux matters; gammas useless (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

DM annihilation produces only protons (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Gamma rays from DM annihilation: spectrum depends on final state. $\chi\chi \to b\bar b$: continuum with $E_{\rm max} = m_\chi$. $\chi\chi \to \gamma\gamma$: monochromatic line — very distinctive but loop-suppressed. Best targets: dwarf spheroidal galaxies (high mass-to-light ratio $\sim 1000$, low background) — Fermi-LAT stacked analysis constrains $\langle\sigma v\rangle < 3\times 10^{-26}$ cm$^3$/s for $m_\chi < $ few 100 GeV (right at thermal relic value!). Galactic centre: 'GeV excess' (Goodenough-Hooper 2009), spectrum consistent with $\sim 30-40$ GeV DM $\to b\bar b$ at thermal cross-section, but also consistent with millisecond pulsars. Antiprotons in AMS-02: small excess at $\sim 10$ GeV but contentious. Neutrinos from Sun: DM captured + annihilates → $\nu$; IceCube limits. Recent: $H\to\gamma\gamma$ — heating CMB, sets HDE constraints.

question: Neutrino floor in DM direct detection: what is it and when do we hit it?

options:
Coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering ($
u N 	o 
u N$) is irreducible background mimicking DM. Solar $^8$B neutrinos contribute below $\sim 6$ GeV DM mass; atmospheric/DSNB above. Current LZ at $7	imes 10^{-48}$ cm$^2$ approaching solar $
u$ floor; future DARWIN/XLZD multi-tonne Xe targets, plus directional detectors (CYGNUS) needed to push below
Neutrino floor is artificial limit; can be bypassed easily (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Neutrinos don't interact with nuclei (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Floor at $\sigma \sim 10^{-30}$ cm$^2$, not yet relevant (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation:
CEvNS ($
u$-nucleus coherent scattering) cross section: $\sigma \sim G_F^2 N^2 E_
u^2$. Solar $^8$B (~14 MeV): nuclear recoils $\sim 1-10$ keV — overlaps low-mass DM signal. Atmospheric/DSNB (~10-100 MeV): higher recoil, overlaps with WIMPs. 'Floor' a fuzzy concept — not absolute limit but the cross-section below which $\sigma$ improvement requires factor $\sim 100$ exposure increase due to neutrino background subtraction. Current generation (LZ, XENONnT): $\sim 7	imes 10^{-48}$ cm$^2$ at 30 GeV, approaching floor in 5-10 yr. Strategies to go below: (1) annual modulation (DM modulates differently from solar $
u$), (2) directional detectors (CYGNUS, NEWS-G — tracks nuclear recoil direction), (3) larger detectors (DARWIN/XLZD 50t Xe, ARGO 300t Ar). CEvNS itself first observed by COHERENT (2017) at SNS spallation source.

question: Axion dark matter: detection principles and current experimental landscape.

options: Axion-photon coupling $\mathcal L = g_{a\gamma}a\mathbf E\cdot\mathbf B$ enables Primakoff conversion in magnetic field; haloscopes (ADMX, MADMAX, ABRACADABRA) tune resonant cavity to $\nu = m_a c^2/h$ (microwave for $\mu$eV mass). Helioscopes (CAST, IAXO) detect solar axions. Light-shining-through-walls (ALPS). Cosmological signatures: spin precession (CASPEr), domain walls. Current ADMX excludes DFSZ-coupling axions in narrow $\mu$eV bands

Axion DM cannot be detected experimentally (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

Only direct nuclear recoil works, same as WIMPs (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general)

Axions detected by gravitational telescopes (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Axion produced by Peccei-Quinn mechanism solving strong-CP problem. DM if produced cold via misalignment mechanism in early universe — coherent classical field oscillating at frequency $\omega = m_a c^2/\hbar$. Haloscope: convert axion DM to photons in static magnetic field via Primakoff-like process; resonant cavity tuned to $\omega$, signal photon rate $\sim g_{a\gamma}^2 \rho_{\rm DM} B^2 V Q/\omega^4$. ADMX (Univ Washington): scans 0.7-1.4 GHz, excludes DFSZ coupling in window; expanding to broader range. MADMAX (Munich): dielectric haloscope for $\sim 100\,\mu$eV. ABRACADABRA, DM Radio: lumped-element for sub-$\mu$eV. ALPS-II (DESY): light shining through wall, 5.3 T over 100 m. IAXO (proposed): helioscope, 1000$\times$ CAST sensitivity. CASPEr: nuclear spin precession in DM background. Recently: high-Q superconducting cavities, quantum sensors push sensitivity orders of magnitude.
